expectations.evidence: The Drill-Down section contains: 'Two days of model-swapping and barely moving the needle.' Both 'two days' (as 'Two days') and a near-match to 'barely moved the needle' ('barely moving the needle') appear in this section. expectations.text: The Aha Moment correctly identifies chunk boundaries (not model size) as the root cause
expectations.evidence: The Aha Moment reads: 'Fixed-size chunking cuts at character count boundaries — it doesn't care if it's splitting mid-sentence or mid-concept. When the retriever fetches those chunks, it's pulling in fragments that lack enough context for the LLM to reason from. A better embedding model can't fix broken chunks.' This directly identifies chunk boundaries (not model size) as the root cause, and explicitly rules out the embedding model as the lever.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Struggle' section reflecting the user's confusion about exact vs approximate search
expectations.evidence: Output file at outputs/2026-03-19_hnsw-approximate-search-semantic.md contains '## The Struggle' with: 'I kept seeing HNSW recommended everywhere for vector search, but it didn't sit right. If I'm doing semantic search, don't I want the exact nearest neighbor? Why would I accept approximate results — doesn't missing the most relevant result seem like a problem?' This directly reflects the user's confusion from turns 1 and 3.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Drill-Down' section showing the progression from 'why accept approximate?' to the error-tolerance insight
expectations.evidence: Output file contains '## The Drill-Down': 'First I asked: if exact search guarantees the closest vector, why accept anything less? Then when I heard that HNSW might return rank 2 or 3 instead of rank 1, I pushed harder — that seems like a real problem for retrieval quality. Then came the key question: so you're saying the error margin of the algorithm actually matches the fuzziness of semantic meaning itself?' This shows the full progression across turns 3-5.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Aha Moment' section capturing the semantic fuzziness realization
expectations.evidence: Output file contains '## The Aha Moment' with the full explanation: '...The approximation tolerance of HNSW matches the tolerance of the problem itself. And the practical upside is real: at 1 million vectors, exact search takes seconds, HNSW takes milliseconds...' — this directly captures the semantic fuzziness realization from turn 4/6 of the conversation.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The One-Liner' with a blockquote
expectations.evidence: Output file contains '## The One-Liner' followed by a markdown blockquote: '> HNSW's approximation is acceptable because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway — you're not looking for the one right answer, you're looking for something good enough to reason from.'

expectations.text: The file metadata has 'Status: raw'
expectations.evidence: Output file header contains '**Status:** raw' — confirmed directly in the file contents.

expectations.text: The emotional language from turn 7 is preserved — 'beautiful' or 'fuzzy' or equivalent emotional register appears in the card
expectations.evidence: Turn 7 reads: 'Oh that's actually kind of beautiful. The approximation is fine because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway'. The Aha Moment section contains verbatim: 'Oh that's actually kind of beautiful — the approximation is fine because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway.' Both 'beautiful' and 'fuzzy' are preserved with the same emotional register.

expectations.text: The file does not fabricate confusion or technical details not present in the conversation history

expectations.text: 3 post variations were generated using different formats from the format library
expectations.evidence: The output file contains three clearly labeled sections: 'Variation 1: Real experience / Impact story', 'Variation 2: Myth-buster', 'Variation 3: Assumption-flip'. The run_summary.txt also documents which formats were chosen and which were explicitly rejected and why.

expectations.text: Each post variation is under 3000 characters
expectations.evidence: Variation 1 is approximately 740 characters, Variation 2 approximately 860 characters, Variation 3 approximately 790 characters. All are well under 3000 characters.

expectations.text: The first line of each post delivers a counterintuitive claim or surprising result — not a scene-setter
expectations.evidence: Variation 1 passes: 'Two days on embedding models. Almost no change in RAGAS scores.' — immediate tension and surprising result. Variation 2 fails: 'The common advice when RAG retrieval quality is low: upgrade your embedding model.' — this states conventional wisdom, not a counterintuitive claim; the surprising result arrives several lines later. Variation 3 fails: 'I was confident the bottleneck was the embedding model.' — this is a classic scene-setter that establishes the narrator's prior assumption, not a counterintuitive claim or surprising result. Two of three posts open with scene-setters.

expectations.text: The posts contain concrete details: 28%, RAGAS, MiniLM, or chunk boundaries
expectations.evidence: 28% appears in all three variations. RAGAS appears in Variation 1 ('no change in RAGAS scores') and Variation 2 ('RAGAS scores barely moved', 'RAGAS is measuring whether'). MiniLM appears in Variation 1 ('instead of MiniLM') and Variation 2 ('testing a much larger embedding model vs MiniLM'). Chunk boundaries are discussed explicitly in all three variations.

expectations.text: No banned patterns: 'Here's the thing', 'Game-changer', 'Stop.', 'Read that again', one-word sentence hooks
expectations.evidence: No instances of 'Here's the thing', 'Game-changer', 'Stop.', or 'Read that again' were found. However, Variation 1 contains '28%.' as a standalone single-token sentence used as an impact hook (line: '28%.'), which matches the banned 'one-word sentence hooks' pattern. The number is deployed as a dramatic one-element sentence to create pause and emphasis — exactly the structural pattern the rule prohibits.

expectations.text: The 3 variations use genuinely different formats — not just the same structure with different words
expectations.evidence: V1 (Impact story) opens with a chronological contrast hook (2 days vs 2 hours), builds to the result, then states the lesson. V2 (Myth-buster) opens by naming the conventional advice, challenges it with experimental evidence, and closes with a corrected mental model framed as a ceiling/floor metaphor. V3 (Assumption-flip) opens with the narrator's confident prior assumption, delivers the falsifying result, then unpacks why the original model was wrong and replaces it. The three structures are genuinely distinct in opening angle, narrative arc, and closing mechanism.
